description: Fried ripe plantain — caramelised, golden, slightly crispy on the outside, soft and sweet inside. Served as a side, a snack, or a party staple. The ripeness of the plantain and the oil temperature are the entire game.

ingredients.name: ripe plantain
ingredients.note: Ripeness is everything. The skin should be mostly black — at least 70% black with some yellow. Fully yellow plantain is not sweet enough and will not caramelise properly. Bright green plantain is for boiling, not frying.

ingredients.name: vegetable oil
ingredients.note: Enough oil to come at least halfway up the plantain pieces in the pan. Do not use too little — the plantain will stick and cook unevenly.

ingredients.name: salt
ingredients.note: Some Nigerians add a tiny pinch of salt to the plantain before frying — it heightens the sweetness. Optional but worth trying.

steps.title: Select and Peel the Plantain
steps.instruction: Before you touch the knife, do the ripeness check — this single assessment determines everything that follows. Press each plantain gently along its length with your fingers; it should feel noticeably soft and yielding, not firm or resistant like a banana you have not yet eaten. The skin should be at least 70% black with some yellow remaining. Peel each plantain by slicing off both ends with a knife, making one shallow cut along the length of the skin without cutting into the flesh, and peeling the skin back in sections. The exposed flesh should be deep yellow to orange in colour — not pale, not white. Pale, firm flesh means the plantain is not ripe enough; set it aside and wait another day.
micro_steps: Pick up each plantain and press it firmly along its length — it should feel soft and give under gentle pressure
Check the skin colour — at least 70% of the skin surface should be black, with the rest yellow
If any plantain feels firm or has mostly yellow skin, set it aside — it is not ready
Using a knife, slice off the top and bottom ends of each ripe plantain
Make one shallow cut along the full length of the skin — cut only through the skin, not the flesh
Peel the skin back in sections, working along the cut
Look at the flesh — it should be deep yellow to orange, moist, and slightly sticky
If the flesh is pale or white, the plantain is not ripe enough — do not proceed with it
prep_notes: Buy plantains 2–3 days before cooking if they are still mostly yellow — leave them at room temperature and they will ripen naturally
Do not refrigerate unripe plantains — cold air stops the ripening process entirely
Have paper towels ready near the stove for draining the finished dodo
If cooking multiple batches, keep the finished ones uncovered in a warm oven at the lowest setting — never cover them or they will go soggy
steps.sensory_cue: Ripe plantain flesh is deep yellow to orange, fragrant, and slightly sticky to the touch. It should smell unmistakably sweet — like a very ripe banana with more depth and warmth. If it smells of nothing or smells starchy, it needs more ripening time. Trust your nose as much as your eyes.
steps.failure_risk: Under-ripe plantain will not caramelise no matter how long or how hot you fry it — it will come out pale, starchy, and bland with no sweetness whatsoever. There is no technique that rescues under-ripe plantain; the ripeness must be right before you start. This is the one non-negotiable in this entire recipe.

steps.title: Cut the Plantain
steps.instruction: Cut each peeled plantain into diagonal slices about 1.5–2cm thick — hold the knife at a 45-degree angle to the plantain rather than cutting straight across. Diagonal cuts expose more surface area to the hot oil, which means more caramelisation and a more visually appealing, elegant shape. Alternatively, cut into straight rounds if you prefer a rounder shape — thicker rounds give a softer, creamier interior while thinner ones give a crispier result throughout. Thickness in the 1.5–2cm range is the sweet spot for the classic dodo texture. If using salt, sprinkle a tiny pinch over all the cut pieces and toss lightly.
micro_steps: Place a peeled plantain on the cutting board
Hold your knife at a 45-degree angle to the plantain — not straight across
Cut into diagonal slices, each approximately 1.5–2cm thick
Aim for consistent thickness across all slices — uneven pieces cook at different rates
Repeat with the remaining plantains
If using salt, sprinkle a very small pinch — no more than ⅛ tsp — over all the cut pieces
Toss lightly to distribute the salt
Set the cut pieces aside in a single layer — do not stack them or they will stick together
steps.sensory_cue: The cut surfaces should look moist and deeply yellow-orange, almost glistening. You can smell the sweetness strongly the moment the flesh is exposed to air — a warm, banana-sweet aroma with a slight floral note from the ripeness.
steps.failure_risk: Cutting slices thinner than 1cm means they will crisp all the way through before the interior has time to get soft and creamy — you lose the contrast that makes dodo special. Cutting thicker than 2.5cm risks the outside browning and caramelising before the inside is fully soft. Stay in the 1.5–2cm range.

steps.title: Heat the Oil
steps.instruction: Pour enough vegetable oil into a wide, heavy pan to come at least halfway up the height of your plantain slices — this is important for even frying and preventing sticking. Place over medium-high heat and let the oil heat for 2–3 minutes. Test the temperature before adding any plantain: drop a single small piece or a tiny scrap of plantain into the oil. If it sinks and sits on the bottom without sizzling, the oil is not hot enough — wait another minute. If it sizzles actively and floats to the surface within a second, the temperature is right. If it turns dark brown in under 10 seconds, the oil is too hot — reduce the heat and wait 30 seconds before testing again.
micro_steps: Pour 1 cup of vegetable oil into your widest, heaviest frying pan
The oil should come at least halfway up the side of your plantain slices when laid flat — add more if needed
Place over medium-high heat
Heat for 2–3 minutes without touching
After 2 minutes, look at the oil — it should shimmer and move fluidly when you tilt the pan slightly
Drop a tiny scrap of plantain into the oil as a temperature test
If it sinks and sits silently — oil is too cold, wait 1 more minute
If it sizzles immediately and floats to the surface — temperature is correct, start frying
If it turns dark in under 10 seconds — oil is too hot, reduce heat and wait 30 seconds before retesting
steps.sensory_cue: Properly heated oil shimmers visibly and moves like a living thing when you tilt the pan — not still and flat like cold oil. The test piece of plantain should sizzle actively, audibly, and float to the surface rather than sinking. The oil should not be smoking — if it smokes, it is too hot.
steps.failure_risk: Oil that is too cold causes the plantain to absorb it like a sponge instead of frying — you end up with greasy, heavy, oil-soaked dodo that is unpleasant to eat. Getting the oil to the right temperature before adding the plantain is the one technical skill in this recipe, and the test piece method makes it foolproof.

steps.title: Fry the First Side
steps.instruction: Lower the plantain slices into the hot oil carefully one at a time, placing them in a single uncrowded layer — leave a little space between each piece. Work in batches if your pan is not wide enough to hold all the pieces with space between them. Once in the oil, leave the plantain completely undisturbed for 2–3 minutes. Do not nudge, press, or move the pieces. Watch the edges of each slice — they will start turning golden, then deepen to a rich caramel-brown as the natural sugars in the ripe plantain caramelise in the hot oil. This is exactly what you want.
micro_steps: Lower the first plantain slice into the oil carefully — tilt it away from you as you place it to avoid oil splashes
Add the remaining pieces one at a time in a single uncrowded layer
If all slices do not fit with space between them, set the excess aside for the next batch
Once all pieces are in, leave them completely undisturbed — do not touch, press, or move them
Watch the edges — after about 90 seconds you should see them starting to turn golden
After 2–3 minutes, the edges and underside should be a deep golden-brown
If the edges are still pale after 3 minutes, the oil may be slightly cool — increase heat a touch
steps.sensory_cue: The sizzling should be consistent and active from the moment the plantain goes in — a steady, energetic sound, not a faint murmur. The edges of the slices will turn from yellow to golden to a rich caramel-brown as the sugars cook. The kitchen will start smelling of warm caramelised sweetness — one of the most recognisable and irresistible aromas in Nigerian cooking.
steps.failure_risk: Moving or pressing the plantain too early breaks the caramelised crust before it has set and cooled slightly — you lose the golden surface and end up with sticky, pale, broken pieces. Patience here is the skill. Leave the pieces completely alone until the edges are clearly and visibly golden.

steps.title: Flip and Fry the Second Side
steps.instruction: When the first side is deep golden-brown, flip each piece carefully using tongs or a fork — slide the tongs under the piece cleanly and turn in one smooth motion. The cooked side should be a rich, even golden-brown — caramelised and glossy. If it looks pale, give it another 30 seconds before flipping. Fry the second side for 1.5–2 minutes — it will cook faster than the first side because the oil is hotter and the plantain is already partially cooked through. Remove each piece with a slotted spoon the moment both sides are evenly golden and drain on paper towels. If frying in batches, taste one piece from the first batch and adjust the heat if subsequent pieces are browning too quickly.
micro_steps: After 2–3 minutes, check the first side by gently lifting one piece with tongs — it should be deep golden-brown
If it looks pale, return it and give it another 30–45 seconds
Flip each piece with tongs in one smooth, confident motion — a hesitant flip splashes hot oil
The flipped side should reveal a deep, even golden-brown caramelised surface
Fry the second side for 1.5–2 minutes — watch it more closely as it cooks faster
When both sides are evenly golden-brown, remove each piece with a slotted spoon
Place immediately on paper towels to drain
Taste a piece from the first batch before frying the next — if it is browning too fast, reduce heat slightly
steps.sensory_cue: Both sides should be a deep, even golden-brown with a slightly glossy caramelised surface. The edges may be a touch darker — that is correct and adds a slightly crisp bite. Press a piece gently with your finger — the inside should feel soft and yielding, not firm. The smell should be intensely sweet and caramelised, like toffee with a fruity edge.
steps.failure_risk: The second side cooks significantly faster than the first because the oil temperature has risen and the plantain is already partially cooked through — it can go from golden to dark brown to burnt in under 30 seconds if you are not watching. Do not walk away from the pan during the second side. Reduce heat slightly for subsequent batches if needed.

steps.title: Drain and Serve
steps.instruction: Lift each piece of dodo from the oil with a slotted spoon and place directly on a plate or tray lined with paper towels. Let them rest for 1–2 minutes — the paper towels will absorb the excess surface oil and the exterior will firm up slightly as it cools just enough to handle. Serve immediately while hot — dodo is at its absolute best in the first few minutes out of the oil, when the caramelised exterior is still slightly crispy and the interior is soft, sweet, and creamy. It softens progressively as it sits and loses the textural contrast that makes it so good. Serve alongside rice and stew, beans, or on its own as a snack.
micro_steps: Line a plate or tray with two layers of paper towels before you start frying — have it ready
Lift each cooked piece from the oil with a slotted spoon, letting excess oil drip back into the pan for a second
Place pieces on the paper towels in a single layer — do not stack
Leave to drain for 1–2 minutes
Do not cover the dodo at any point — trapped steam softens the exterior completely
Transfer to a serving plate and serve immediately while hot
If making multiple batches, keep the first batch uncovered in a warm oven at the lowest temperature setting while the rest fry
steps.sensory_cue: Perfect dodo is deep golden-brown with a caramelised, slightly glossy surface. When you bite in, the exterior gives way cleanly to a soft, sweet, creamy interior. The caramel smell should be strong and appetising — warm, sweet, and distinctly plantain. The contrast between the slight crispness of the exterior and the softness inside is the signature of well-made dodo.
steps.failure_risk: Covering or stacking hot dodo immediately traps the steam from the interior and turns the crispy exterior completely soft within minutes — you lose the textural contrast that makes it worth eating fresh. Keep it uncovered and in a single layer at all times. Eat it as soon as possible after frying.
